quiz_questions.question_text: A strong acid:

options.text: Completely dissociates in water
Is always very concentrated
Does not dissociate at all
Only partially dissociates

quiz_questions.explanation: A strong acid completely dissociates (ionizes) in water, meaning 100% of its molecules break apart into ions. Examples include HCl, HNO₃, and H₂SO₄.

quiz_questions.question_text: A weak acid:

options.text: Never dissociates in water
Completely dissociates in water
Is always very dilute
Partially dissociates in water

quiz_questions.explanation: A weak acid only partially dissociates in water, meaning only some of its molecules break apart into ions. Examples include acetic acid (CH₃COOH) and hydrofluoric acid (HF).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these is a strong acid?

options.text: HF (hydrofluoric acid)
CH₃COOH (acetic acid)
HCl (hydrochloric acid)
H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid)

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl (hydrochloric acid) is a strong acid that completely dissociates in water. HF, CH₃COOH, and H₂CO₃ are all weak acids that only partially dissociate.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these is a weak acid?

options.text: Acetic acid (CH₃COOH)
Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)
Nitric acid (HNO₃)
Hydrochloric acid (HCl)

quiz_questions.explanation: Acetic acid (CH₃COOH), found in vinegar, is a weak acid that only partially dissociates in water. H₂SO₄, HNO₃, and HCl are all strong acids that completely dissociate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A strong base:

options.text: Completely dissociates in water
Does not dissolve in water
Is always a weak electrolyte
Only partially dissociates

quiz_questions.explanation: A strong base completely dissociates in water, producing 100% of its possible ions. NaOH and KOH are common examples of strong bases.

quiz_questions.question_text: NaOH is classified as a:

options.text: Weak base
Strong acid
Weak acid
Strong base

quiz_questions.explanation: NaOH (sodium hydroxide) is a strong base because it completely dissociates into Na⁺ and OH⁻ ions in water. It is commonly known as lye or caustic soda.

quiz_questions.question_text: Strength vs. concentration: a dilute strong acid:

options.text: Is fully dissociated but has few moles of acid per liter
Is only partially dissociated in the water solution
Cannot exist because strong acids are always concentrated
Is not considered an acid because it is too dilute

quiz_questions.explanation: Strength and concentration are different concepts. A dilute strong acid is fully dissociated (100% ionized) but has a small number of moles of acid per liter of solution. Strength refers to ionization, not amount.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a strong acid?

options.text: H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid)
HNO₃ (nitric acid)
HF (hydrofluoric acid)
H₃PO₄ (phosphoric acid)

quiz_questions.explanation: HNO₃ (nitric acid) is one of the common strong acids that completely dissociates in water. H₂CO₃, HF, and H₃PO₄ are all weak acids that only partially dissociate.

quiz_questions.question_text: NH₃ (ammonia) dissolved in water is classified as a:

options.text: Strong acid
Strong base
Weak base
Weak acid

quiz_questions.explanation: NH₃ (ammonia) is a weak base that only partially accepts protons from water. It does not completely ionize, so it is classified as weak rather than strong.

quiz_questions.question_text: H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid) is classified as a:

options.text: Weak acid
Weak base
Strong base
Strong acid

quiz_questions.explanation: H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid) is a strong acid that completely dissociates in water. It is one of the most commonly used strong acids in chemistry laboratories.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'complete dissociation' mean for a strong acid?

options.text: Only half of the acid molecules break apart into ions
100% of the acid molecules break apart into ions in water
The acid molecules dissolve but stay as whole molecules
The acid evaporates completely when added to water

quiz_questions.explanation: Complete dissociation means that 100% of the acid molecules separate into their component ions when dissolved in water. No intact acid molecules remain in solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'partial dissociation' mean for a weak acid?

options.text: All molecules break apart into ions in water solution
Only some molecules break apart into ions while the rest remain intact
No molecules break apart and the acid stays undissolved
The acid molecules split into atoms rather than ions

quiz_questions.explanation: Partial dissociation means only a fraction of the acid molecules separate into ions. The remaining molecules stay intact as undissociated acid. This is characteristic of all weak acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between acid strength and acid concentration?

options.text: They mean exactly the same thing in chemistry
Strength is degree of ionization; concentration is moles per liter
Strength is moles per liter; concentration is degree of ionization
Concentration only applies to bases, not to acids

quiz_questions.explanation: Strength refers to how completely an acid ionizes (strong = 100%, weak = partial). Concentration refers to how many moles of acid are dissolved per liter of solution. A strong acid can be dilute, and a weak acid can be concentrated.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define 'strong electrolyte' in the context of acids and bases.

options.text: A substance that does not conduct electricity in water
A substance that fully ionizes and conducts electricity well in water
A substance that partially ionizes and conducts poorly
A substance that only conducts electricity when heated

quiz_questions.explanation: A strong electrolyte fully ionizes in water and conducts electricity well because many free ions are present. Strong acids and strong bases are strong electrolytes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a weak electrolyte?

options.text: A substance that fully ionizes and conducts electricity well
A substance that partially ionizes and conducts electricity poorly
A substance that does not dissolve in water at all
A substance that only exists in the solid state of matter

quiz_questions.explanation: A weak electrolyte only partially ionizes in water, producing fewer free ions, so it conducts electricity poorly compared to a strong electrolyte. Weak acids and weak bases are weak electrolytes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does Ka measure for an acid?

options.text: The concentration of the acid in moles per liter
The temperature at which the acid boils and evaporates
The strength of the acid based on its ionization in water
The volume of water needed to dissolve the acid fully

quiz_questions.explanation: Ka is the acid dissociation constant. It measures how much an acid ionizes in water. A larger Ka value means the acid ionizes more, indicating a stronger acid.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean when an acid has a large Ka value?

options.text: The acid is weak and barely ionizes in water
The acid is strong and ionizes extensively in water
The acid is neutral and does not produce any ions
The acid is very dilute with few molecules present

quiz_questions.explanation: A large Ka value indicates the acid ionizes extensively in water, meaning it is a strong acid. A small Ka value means the acid barely ionizes, indicating a weak acid.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'ionization' in acid-base chemistry?

options.text: The process of heating a substance to its melting point
The process of a molecule breaking apart into charged ions
The process of two neutral atoms forming a covalent bond
The process of filtering dissolved particles from water

quiz_questions.explanation: Ionization is the process of a neutral molecule breaking apart into positively and negatively charged ions. In acid-base chemistry, it describes how acids produce H⁺ and bases produce OH⁻.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define 'concentrated acid' versus 'dilute acid.'

options.text: Concentrated means strong; dilute means weak acid strength
Concentrated has many moles per liter; dilute has few moles per liter
Concentrated means the acid is heated; dilute means it is cooled
Concentrated means fully ionized; dilute means partially ionized

quiz_questions.explanation: Concentrated means there are many moles of acid per liter of solution. Dilute means there are few moles per liter. This is different from strength, which describes how much the acid ionizes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'equilibrium' in the context of weak acid dissociation?

options.text: All acid molecules have fully dissociated into ions
The rate of dissociation equals the rate of recombination
No dissociation is occurring in the solution at all
The temperature of the solution has stopped changing

quiz_questions.explanation: In a weak acid solution, equilibrium is reached when the rate of dissociation (breaking apart into ions) equals the rate of recombination (ions rejoining). Both processes continue, but the concentrations remain constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has two beakers: one with 0.1 M HCl and one with 0.1 M CH₃COOH. Both have the same concentration. Which solution has more H⁺ ions?

options.text: CH₃COOH because weak acids produce more H⁺ ions
HCl because it completely dissociates, releasing all its H⁺
Both have the same number of H⁺ ions in solution
Neither produces H⁺ ions at this low concentration

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl is a strong acid that completely dissociates, so all 0.1 M of HCl produces H⁺ ions. CH₃COOH is a weak acid that only partially dissociates, so it produces fewer H⁺ ions at the same concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tests two solutions with a conductivity meter. Solution A lights the bulb brightly, while Solution B barely lights it. Both are acids at the same concentration. What can the student conclude?

options.text: Solution A is a weak acid and Solution B is a strong acid
Solution A is a strong acid and Solution B is a weak acid
Both solutions are strong acids with different temperatures
Both solutions are weak acids with different volumes

quiz_questions.explanation: Strong acids fully ionize and produce many ions, so they conduct electricity well (bright bulb). Weak acids partially ionize with fewer ions, so they conduct poorly (dim bulb). Same concentration rules out dilution as the cause.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has a 2 M solution of acetic acid (weak) and a 0.01 M solution of HCl (strong). She asks: 'Which is the stronger acid?' What is the correct answer?

options.text: Acetic acid is stronger because it is more concentrated
HCl is the stronger acid regardless of its concentration
They are equal in strength since both are acids
Neither is an acid because the concentrations are different

quiz_questions.explanation: Acid strength is about the degree of ionization, not concentration. HCl completely ionizes (strong acid) while acetic acid only partially ionizes (weak acid). Concentration does not change strength.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student adds a piece of magnesium metal to two beakers: one containing 1 M HCl and the other containing 1 M CH₃COOH. She observes faster bubbling in the HCl beaker. Why?

options.text: HCl is hotter than CH₃COOH so the reaction goes faster
HCl produces more H⁺ ions because it fully dissociates
CH₃COOH does not react with metals under any conditions
The magnesium in the HCl beaker is a different metal

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl is a strong acid that fully dissociates, producing more H⁺ ions available to react with magnesium. More H⁺ ions means a faster reaction rate and more hydrogen gas bubbles.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student measures the pH of 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M HF. The HCl has a lower pH. Why is this?

options.text: HCl has a higher molecular weight than HF does
HCl fully dissociates, producing more H⁺ and lowering pH
HF fully dissociates while HCl only partially does
pH measurements are unreliable for comparing two acids

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl is a strong acid (fully dissociates), while HF is a weak acid (partially dissociates). At the same concentration, HCl produces more H⁺ ions, resulting in a lower pH value.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is confused about why 6 M acetic acid (weak) can still cause burns, even though it is a weak acid. Which explanation is correct?

options.text: Weak acids cannot cause burns under any conditions at all
A concentrated weak acid has enough H⁺ ions to be corrosive
The acetic acid has become a strong acid at high concentration
Only the container is dangerous, not the acid solution itself

quiz_questions.explanation: Even though acetic acid is weak (partially ionizes), a highly concentrated solution still produces enough H⁺ ions to be corrosive. Concentration and strength are independent properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is asked to list the six common strong acids. Which list is correct?

options.text: HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, HClO₄
HCl, HF, HBr, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃
HCl, CH₃COOH, HBr, HNO₃, H₃PO₄, HI
NaOH, KOH, HCl, HBr, HNO₃, H₂SO₄

quiz_questions.explanation: The six common strong acids are HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, and HClO₄. HF is a weak acid despite being a hydrohalic acid. NaOH and KOH are strong bases, not acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student mixes a strong base (NaOH) and a weak base (NH₃) separately with water. She notices NaOH conducts electricity much better. Why?

options.text: NaOH is lighter than NH₃ so it dissolves more quickly
NaOH fully dissociates, producing more ions than NH₃ does
NH₃ is not actually a base and produces no ions at all
NaOH is an acid that conducts better than any base

quiz_questions.explanation: NaOH is a strong base that completely dissociates into Na⁺ and OH⁻ ions. NH₃ is a weak base that only partially ionizes. More ions in solution means better electrical conductivity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is studying for a test and wants to know: can a weak acid be more corrosive than a dilute strong acid? What is the answer?

options.text: No, strong acids are always more corrosive than weak acids
Yes, a concentrated weak acid can have more H⁺ than a very dilute strong acid
No, weak acids never produce any H⁺ ions in solution
Yes, but only if the weak acid is heated above 100 degrees

quiz_questions.explanation: Yes, a concentrated weak acid can produce more total H⁺ ions than a very dilute strong acid. For example, 10 M acetic acid has more H⁺ than 0.0001 M HCl, even though HCl is the stronger acid.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student draws a particle diagram showing a beaker where all acid molecules are separated into ions. Another diagram shows a beaker where only some molecules are separated. Which diagram represents a weak acid?

options.text: The diagram where all molecules are separated into ions
The diagram where only some molecules are separated into ions
Neither diagram represents an acid of any kind
Both diagrams represent the exact same type of acid

quiz_questions.explanation: The diagram showing only some molecules separated into ions represents a weak acid (partial dissociation). The diagram with all molecules separated shows a strong acid (complete dissociation).
